_doc: Canonical claims promoted from data/claim-candidates.jsonl. Each claim has a stable slug, a status (working / load-bearing / provisional / retired), a summary, and one or more anchors in `harvestedFrom` (used by the build's drift check to flag stale anchors when the underlying prose changes). `argues` lists notes that advance the claim; `arguedInChapters` lists chapters; `dependsOn` lists other claims (slug-only or `claim:slug`) or questions (`question:slug`); `supersedes` lists prior-version claims. Run `make harvest` to refresh the candidate inbox; promote new candidates by editing this file.

claims.label: Transport and selection are parallel mechanisms
claims.summary: Transport (lossy re-encoding) and selection (gates with local criteria) are parallel mechanisms operating in series at every stage of the abstraction pipeline. Neither is downstream of the other. Replaces the original 'which is primary' framing.

harvestedFrom.text: both are operating, and the output of each stage is a small fraction of the input, in heavily transformed form.
both are real, parallel mechanisms operating in series at every stage. The "is one primary?" framing was wrong.

claims.label: Selection is primary
claims.summary: Of the two parallel mechanisms, selection is the primary determinant of what propagates. Transport is real but increasingly downstream — a substrate, not the dynamic.

harvestedFrom.text: Selection is primary. Transport is real but increasingly downstream.

claims.label: Transport-cost collapse shifts the binding constraint
claims.summary: In modern digital networks the cost of transport has collapsed to near zero, so selection becomes the dominant constraint on what spreads. In earlier eras with expensive transit, transport was a binding constraint and a transport-style argument had real force. The relevant constraint has shifted as the medium has changed.

harvestedFrom.text: In modern digital networks, the cost of transport has collapsed to near zero, so selection becomes the dominant constraint on what spreads.

claims.label: Every gate has a medium that shapes its criteria
claims.summary: Every node and every arrow in the two-pipeline picture has a medium attached. The selection criteria at each gate are downstream of what that medium can hold and what it rewards. Changing the medium changes the criteria — the pipeline operates inside a specific media ecosystem.
aliases: the medium is the selection criteria

harvestedFrom.text: every node and every arrow in this two-pipeline picture has a medium attached to it. The selection criteria at each gate are downstream of what that medium can hold and what it rewards.
every gate in the pipeline has a medium attached, and the gate's criteria are downstream of what that medium can hold and what it rewards.

claims.label: Spread is inverse to complexity
claims.summary: How easily a piece of information spreads across a social network is inversely related to how complex it is. The transport half of the two-mechanism structure; pressure-tested by the question of whether 'complexity' is doing two jobs (precondition count vs. handle-ability).

harvestedFrom.text: how easily a piece of information spreads across a social network is inversely related to how complex it is.

claims.label: Complexity ceiling bounded by network size
claims.summary: The maximum complexity an idea can carry is bounded by the size of the network it needs to traverse. Bigger network, lower ceiling. Refined in workshop to include a time-window factor.

harvestedFrom.text: the maximum complexity an idea can carry is bounded by the size of the network it needs to traverse.
the maximum complexity an idea can carry across a network in a given window of time is bounded by the size of

claims.label: Cost of generality is accessibility, and vice versa
claims.summary: Optionality and accessibility trade off: the more options a thing has, the harder it is to access. The cost of generality is accessibility; the cost of accessibility is generality. Same curve appears in software libraries, religions, political platforms, scientific popularization, legal codes.

harvestedFrom.text: They've constrained the user's options for the benefit of increasing overall accessibility.

claims.label: Core truth survives a medium on two joint conditions
claims.summary: Core truth survives a medium to the degree that two things both hold: the medium's gate-criteria reward something correlated with truth, and the medium's want-loop doesn't erode the receiver's appetite for truth over time. Both conditions, not either.

harvestedFrom.text: Core truth survives a medium to the degree that two things hold: the medium's gate-criteria reward something correlated with truth, and the medium's want-loop doesn't erode the receiver's appetite for truth over time.

claims.label: The outer message is what the book calls preconditions
claims.summary: A message's outer message — the decoding mechanism a receiver must already hold to extract the content — is exactly what this book calls preconditions. A complex idea has a large or rare outer message; a compressed idea has one common enough to assume in everyone. The outer message lives in the medium.

harvestedFrom.text: the outer message is the decoding mechanism a receiver must already hold — and that is exactly what this book has been calling preconditions.

claims.label: Transit strips the outer message, not the inner one
claims.summary: What a lossy hop usually strips is the outer message (the decoding key — qualifications, scope conditions, methodology), not the inner message (the claim itself). A surviving inner message then gets decoded with the receiver's default key and yields a false belief. Most of what the book calls distortion is outer-message loss.

harvestedFrom.text: What a lossy hop usually strips is the outer message, not the inner one.

claims.label: Compression strips the decoding key, not truth itself
claims.summary: Compression does not act on a claim's truth value directly. It strips the outer message — the decoding key — and leaves the bare claim intact, so whether truth survives depends entirely on which key the receiver decodes with instead.

harvestedFrom.text: compression does not act on truth directly. It strips the decoding key, and whether truth survives depends entirely on which key the receiver reaches for instead.

claims.label: Truth value lives in the key-fit, not the claim
claims.summary: Truth value is not a property of a claim in isolation; it is a property of the fit between the claim's decoding key and the key its receiver actually uses. This is where truth value enters the pipeline model — the provisional resolution of the truth-value-placement question.
aliases: truth value in the decoding-key fit
where truth value enters the model

harvestedFrom.text: truth value is not a property of a claim; it is a property of the fit between the claim's decoding key and the key its receiver actually uses.

claims.label: Selection picks the truth-compression regime
claims.summary: Transport alone only scrambles — it has no preferred outcome. Inversion and orthogonality are not failures of compression but compressed variants that a selection gate preferred. Which of the three regimes a claim lands in is set by what the gate rewards.

harvestedFrom.text: Inversion and orthogonality are not failures of compression; they are compressed variants that a selection gate preferred.

claims.label: Engagement-tuned gates systematically reward truth-losing compressions
claims.summary: The book's breakage claim, now following from the structure: the failure is not that information gets compressed but that the dominant medium's gates systematically reward the compressions in which truth inverts or stops mattering over the ones in which it survives — because alarm out-competes calibration and a flag out-competes a claim at an engagement-tuned gate. This closes the moral-neutrality gap.
aliases: the breakage is selection for truth-losing compression

harvestedFrom.text: the breakage this book is about is not that information gets compressed — it is that the dominant medium's gates systematically reward the compressions in which truth inverts or stops mattering over the ones in which it survives, because alarm out-competes calibration and a flag out-competes a claim at any gate tuned for engagement.

claims.label: Compression widens interpretive latitude
claims.summary: Stripping a claim's outer message does not fix a single wrong decoding key — it unpins the key, leaving the claim underdetermined. The more compressed the claim, the wider the range of readings it supports across a population. Compression's second effect, alongside shifting the expected decode.

harvestedFrom.text: Stripping an idea's outer message does not pin a wrong decoding key; it unpins the key — and the more of the outer message you strip, the wider the range of readings the surviving claim will support.

claims.label: A myth's reach and its determinacy trade off
claims.summary: A myth simple enough to bind a whole network is necessarily one the network can read many different ways. Reach and determinacy trade off — the same curve as reach-vs-complexity and reach-vs-truth, here as reach-vs-shared-meaning.

harvestedFrom.text: A myth's reach and its determinacy trade off: a myth simple enough for a whole network to hold is, necessarily, one the network can read many different ways.

claims.label: A myth's unity and its fragmentation are one fact
claims.summary: A large network's unifying myth and its latent fragmentation are the same fact seen from two sides: the myth can be universally affirmed precisely because it is vague enough that each segment affirms a different myth. Network segmentation is interpretive latitude sorted into clusters.

harvestedFrom.text: A large network's unifying myth and its latent fragmentation are the same fact seen from two sides — the myth can be universally affirmed precisely because it is vague enough that each segment is affirming a different myth.

claims.label: Bureaucracy is downstream of the myth
claims.summary: Bureaucracy is not a co-equal pillar with myth but the institution a network grows to repair myth dilution — to re-supply the decoding key scaling forced the myth to drop. The larger the network, the more of its cohesion shifts from myth to bureaucracy.

harvestedFrom.text: Bureaucracy is downstream of the myth: it is the institution a network grows to cover the gap that scaling tore open in its myth, and the larger the network, the more of its cohesion has to shift from myth to bureaucracy.

claims.label: The dilution loop's outcome is set by the bureaucracy's resistance to capture
claims.summary: The myth-dilution loop has no fixed destiny. Whether it compounds (safe scale), fragments (a too-weak bureaucracy), or inverts into scale-as-control (a captured-but-effective bureaucracy) is set by the repairing bureaucracy's own resistance to manipulation and capture. A good institution is not one that cannot be captured but one where capture cannot quietly hold.
aliases: loop outcome set by capture resistance

harvestedFrom.text: A good institution is not one that cannot be captured; it is one where capture cannot quietly hold.
